title: Tunnel Clearance
desc: The connecting tunnels are choked with sand. Someone has to dig.

steps.text:
The storm tunnels connect half of Mipedian civilization — trade routes, communication lines, escape passages. When sand clogs them, everything slows. Today, tunnel seven-north is blocked. A sand drift has filled a thirty-foot section floor to ceiling.

The clearance crew is five strong. The foreman surveys the blockage and sighs. "Standard drift. Wind pushed through the upper vents and deposited here. Three hours of shoveling, maybe four."

The tools are simple — flat shovels, wheelbarrows, and a system. One team digs from the front, loading barrows. A second team hauls barrows to the nearest vent shaft and dumps sand back to the surface.

"It's the same sand every time," the foreman says. "We clear it, the wind blows it back. The engineers have been promising vent covers for two years."

options.label: Join the digging team at the front
Join the hauling team — keep the flow moving

steps.text:
Digging the front is the worst job and the most satisfying. You attack the sand wall with your shovel, cutting into the packed drift. It's not loose sand — it's wind-compressed, almost solid, and it breaks in chunks rather than flowing.

Each chunk goes into a wheelbarrow. When the barrow is full, you shove it toward the haulers and grab an empty one. The rhythm is mechanical — dig, load, push, grab, dig.

An hour in, your shovel hits stone. Not the tunnel wall — something buried in the drift. You dig around it. A stone slab, carved with old waymarking symbols. It's a junction marker from the original tunnel construction — meant to hang on the wall but ripped free by years of sand erosion.

The foreman examines it. "This is from the first excavation. Hundred years old, easy." He runs his fingers over the carved symbols. "They used a different notation system. This says... 'storm gallery, south junction, royal passage.' Royal passage. That's not on current maps."

He sets the marker aside. "I'll send it to the archivists. Maybe there's a sealed passage behind this drift." He looks at the remaining sand wall with new interest. "Dig faster."

You dig faster. No sealed passage appears — just more sand and eventually the clear tunnel on the other side. But the marker is real, and somewhere behind these walls, there might be tunnels that nobody has walked in a century.

options.label: Finish the clearance with new motivation

steps.text:
Hauling is straightforward — grab a full barrow, push it to the vent shaft, dump, return with the empty. The shaft is two hundred feet from the dig face, and the tunnel floor is uneven stone. Each trip is a battle against physics.

The barrows are heavy. The wheels catch on every stone lip. You learn the floor — memorizing the bumps, the dips, the spot where the left wheel always catches if you don't angle right.

By the tenth trip, you've found the optimal path. By the twentieth, you can push a full barrow at a jog. The dumping process is its own art — tip the barrow at the vent shaft and let the sand cascade up and out into the desert. Sometimes the wind catches it and blows it right back. You learn to check the wind first.

The team finishes in three hours flat — ahead of schedule. The tunnel is clear, the floor swept, the air flow restored. You can feel the difference immediately — a cool draft from the far end, carrying the faint smell of the catacomb herberies.

The foreman marks the clearance in his logbook. "Three hours. That's a record for seven-north." He looks at the vent shaft. "See you in two weeks when the wind fills it again."

He's not joking. But for now, the tunnel is clear, and the crews who use it will walk freely because someone shoveled sand today.

options.label: Stretch your aching back and call it done
